speaker: interviewer
text: May 3, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So your family all settled here, and you were building your own family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband got his state boards in due time, and he started to intern, which was very-- it was very hard to find an internship in any of the hospitals. But he was lucky. He found one in-- in Brooklyn, Wyckoff Heights hospital. And had a few connections there, so that's how we got in. He was the first foreign intern to be taken in that hospital. And now I don't think you'll find an American intern there anymore. And he had a two year internship, and then he had a residency in Triboro Hospital in lung diseases. And then he finally opened up his own office, I believe, in-- can't remember. Maybe 1930-- that was before the war. 1937, I think. When we came back from Santa Fe, he opened up his own office in Kew Gardens. And it was not a rousing success. It was very hard to, you know, start out with something, and we had no resources to fall back on, of course. We had no money. So he made house calls for other doctors, which kept us in eating money. And a friend-- a fellow who was very friendly with him, a doctor from Wyckoff Heights hospital-- wanted to specialize in surgery only. He had done everything, you know. Then, doctors did everything. They did general practice, and they did surgery, and they-- they deliver babies. But he wanted to specialize in surgery, so he asked my husband if he would like to come and do all the medicine. And, of course, that was great.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, financially, we were-- we were all set. But it was very, very hard because he happened to be a very conscientious doctor. And he was through the last day when he practiced medicine, at the a-- until the age of 84, when he retired, only because he couldn't take the bureaucracy of Medicare anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But his patients-- I always said, first comes medicine, and then later on came golf, and then came us. Or came I, maybe. I don't know. It's a little bit exaggerated. But he-- he was so dedicated to medicine. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those years, were you involved in working at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I took care of my children, took care of the house. It was a very-- disorganized house, because my children would go to school and my husband would still be asleep. And when he came home at night, the children would be asleep, and I would have to cook them dinner at 6:00. And he would come home and eat at 10:00 or 11:00 or 12:00 or 1:00 whatever time he came home. And it was-- it was not a-- a terrific family life, let's put it that way. And I had made up my mind, from the very beginning, that this is the way I had to live because I knew medicine was first in his life. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any athletic activities of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't do much anyway after-- after I, you know, the-- the-- track and field was over, I started to play golf in the 1950s, I think. And then I had a couple of slipped disks that stopped me from playing for awhile. And I kind of grew out of it. It was a little slow for me. It wasn't-- I-- I was very good at it. But I never-- I never took to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to this day, I still bowl twice a week. And I get enough exercise running up and down my stairs here. So I don't do anything. I don't, you know, exercise of any kind-- run or whatever. I don't do any. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel healthy, so why should I push it? You mentioned that there was, more recently, a spate of publicity about your athletic past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Somehow, in 1980, there is a-- a Jewish Sports Hall of Fame that-- I don't know where they're situated now. Then, they were in Los Angeles. And they-- they inducted me in the Jewish Sports Hall of Fame, and that started the whole thing. I mean, all these years, this-- this whole thing was completely dormant. There was not a word. Never heard from Germany, from anybody, and-- But then in 1980, all of a sudden, this whole thing started to-- to take off. The-- some man in-- in-- in Laupheim, whom I never met-- he only moved there much later-- he wrote to me when he saw notice of that in-- in the German newspapers. And he said, I feel that Germany owes you. That was a terrible thing they did to you. And he got in touch with the German Track and Field Association, and they sent me some kind of a medal. It's up there someplace. And it doesn't mean much to me, because I would have refused it if it hadn't been for this man's efforts. I didn't want to insult him. But I-- I didn't want to have anything to do with Germany. They couldn't find to apologize for all these years. I wasn't interested in their medal now either. So-- and I don't know. It just-- everything just snowballed from then on. I was just inducted into the New York Jewish Sports Hall of Fame. I was interviewed by German television twice or three-- three times, I think, altogether. So all of a sudden, I'm a celebrity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe one thing I should go back to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1932, I met a young man, and we fell in love. And he wasn't Jewish. And we really were talking about getting married much later. I mean, in Germany, you didn't get married when you were 18 or 19. You had to wait, you know. The man had to have a good job and all this kind of stuff. And of course, in 1933, we knew there would be no-- no future for us. So we had to break it up, and it was a very, very painful thing to do. And I've-- you know. We met and we said goodbye and that was the end of it. And many years later, I found out he-- he died in-- in the war. He was killed in the-- in-- fighting Hitler's war, which he was very much opposed to anyway. So that was something worthwhile. My mother never knew about it. Even though we had not a very religious house, she would have objected very much if I had married a non-Jewish person. But, to me, it didn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you're recalling those years in Laupheim, what happened to your extended family-- aunts, uncles, cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them got out. Most of them. But many people that I know-- my mother's family, her brother, her cousins-- a lot of them were-- were, you know, taken off to concentration camps. And my husband's parents, we know that they were taken off. And we never heard from them again. And we never researched in what camp they died because we didn't think it made any difference. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband went-- when he was in Germany in the army, after the Allies had go-- gone over the Rhine-- they lived in the Rhineland and he got permission to go to his hometown and research what happened. And he got there, and they told him that they had packed them into the cattle cars and taken them off, at a certain date-- I think in 1944-- and we never heard from them again. So we know what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I said, we never had-- we never had the-- the-- the-- the desire to find out where it happened because, what's the use? You know, they're not there anymore. And that they had to go the same way-- the same horrible way the others had to go. And we just felt--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband is in complete denial of it anyhow. He cannot see anything on television. He can't hear anything on the radio. Whatever has-- he wouldn't watch anything that happened now, after 50 years, you know, concentration camp business. He-- he just can't. He can't deal with it, even-- even to this day. And so as I said, we never tried to find out just where-- where they were killed, and it didn't make any difference anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But many people that we know went the same way. And I just can't imagine that somebody-- you know, some country was supposed to be cultured and, you know, all the culture that they have and sophisticated-- that-- that it could happen in a country like this. But it did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, those people that were killed in the concentration camps and the ones that survived the concentration camps, they went through much more than we did. But the psychological scars that-- that we who survived got-- whether you survived the concentration camp or whether you weren't even near anything like this-- I-- I think they're still with us. When I came over here, I-- I was so afraid of everything. If I saw somebody in a uniform, I would start to shake. And I would walk up and down in front of a cafeteria for about 20 minutes before I had the nerve to go inside and-- and, you know, eat because I was afraid of everything. So that all I had to do with-- even though, you know, over there, I was this big track star and everything, over here, I was like a-- I felt like nothing. I felt like-- completely inferior and I was just afraid of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about this recent belated recognition?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's kind of funny, really. I mean, I don't feel that I'm-- I'm not flattered by it or anything. Because I feel what I did then, I only did because I was talented. I had a natural talent for-- for athletics. I didn't work at it. I mean, when I see what the-- the athletes do today, they run so many miles a day, and they do weightlifting, and they do this, and they do that. And they do a little steroid too, so they do well. Today, it's not what it was then. Today, it's strictly a money-making proposition. I mean, these people who get into the Olympics, they can make a lot of money, and I think that's-- that's their incentive. For us, it was just plain fun to do it. If you won, fine. I never lost, so I don't know how it felt to lose. But it was just fun to do. And it was a social thing, and you practiced a little bit. And then you went into the clubhouse, and-- and you had fun. And that was-- that was the whole thing. And the recognition in itself, I really-- it doesn't mean all that much to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have some thoughts about how the Holocaust developed?

speaker: interviewee
text: How-- well, how can you think about it? I mean, it's so unthinkable that anything like this, in-- in today's world, can happen. But then you look at Bosnia, you look at other-- you look at-- wherever you look, there is this-- this-- this killing going on. And I think it can happen anyplace. It can even happen in the United States. And when you know what goes on-- I mean, Oklahoma City and all this business. If you get enough nutty people to get together and one person who is charismatic or whatever, it can happen anyplace, I think, even in the United States. And I'm glad I'm old, and I don't have to go through anything like this again, because it could happen, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think of leaving your life story [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for your future generations, is there anything more that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to say I have been asked in-- to speak to a couple of sixth-- grader classes after-- just recently. And the only thing I could tell them was that if you-- you know, speaking to the children-- if you meet another child that's different from you, don't hate that child just because it might be black-skinned, or yellow-skinned or whatever. Don't hate anybody by what they look like. You don't have to li-- like the whole world. You don't have to like everybody. But don't make an opinion about their appearance. They can be disabled, too. And-- and kids are very cruel. They-- they might think, you know, well, this kid can't run with me, so I don't want to have anything to do with him. Just don't judge anybody before you get to know them well. And that's the same thing with everybody. You meet somebody, and you might-- you might form an opinion before you ever get to know them. And it's terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You speak to young people now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whenever--

speaker: interviewer
text: And use yourself as an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I said, I was discriminated against, and it hurts a great deal. And don't do it to anybody else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I said, you don't have to like everybody. But don't do it because you think that this is not a good person because it looks different from you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been open in sharing all this with your children, too--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you lived through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. As a matter of fact, they-- they made me-- I'm-- I'm in the process of writing a book. I mean, it's a-- a very long pregnancy with that book. But they kept saying, why don't you write all this stuff down? Because it's really interesting. And even if the book never gets published, I-- I'm not very compulsive about it anyway. If it doesn't get published, it's just a legacy for my children and my grandchildren. I just sent them a book. My name is in a bunch of book, too, you know. This-- this really has been an international kind of thing. And I have books upstairs where my name is. And somebody wrote a book, and my name is in. And I just send it to my grandchildren. I said, it's good for you to-- to know what has been going on. And my granddaughter, I think, told her teacher once. And the teacher took the book, and he spoke to the class about it. And my granddaughter said, I was very proud of you. [LAUGHS] But I think young people should know. They must know what it's like. Even-- even in-- in her-- in their school, I don't think there are any-- any black children, for instance. And I'm very sorry about it, because I think you have to learn to get along with everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I hope it, you know, helps. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father's father in his office in Laupheim, in his factory. And his name is Anton Bergmann. And it must have been taken before 1914, because I never knew him. He died before I was born. And he-- he and a brother came from Czechoslovakia and founded this factory, which is still in existence today, although it's-- I don't believe it's in Jewish hands anymore. My cousin kept it up for a while, but then I don't think-- he died, and I don't think it's in German-- in-- in Jewish hands anymore. This is a picture of my mother, Paula, who was born in Frankfurt am Main and, in later years, always hated to have her picture taken and always hid her face behind her hands when somebody wanted to take a photograph. And she was a very bright lady, but she-- she didn't enjoy her life because she was full of complexes of some kind. She thought she was the ugly duckling in the family and all this kind of stuff. This is a picture of my father, Edwin, who-- I think the picture most likely was taken in England after they-- they got to England after 1940. And he was a very funny fellow, had a terrific sense of humor. And when-- before they went to England, they all had some kind of a-- a card to carry to-- that they be recognized as Jews. They had a Jewish star they had to wear. And all the men, in their passports, and wherever were called, first name Edwin Israel Bergmann. The women were called Sara. So my mother would have been Paula Sara Bergmann. I don't know why they did that, but I think they-- that the Nazis thought that was some kind of an insult if you called somebody Israel or Sara. This is a picture of the papers that the Jews had to carry all the time, and the Jewish star on top, which they had to wear after a certain time. I don't know when that was made a law. And the name-- my father's name was Edwin Bergmann. He had no middle name, but the Nazis named everybody. Everybody had to take the middle name of Israel. The men had to take the name-- the middle name of Israel, and the women had to take the middle name of Sara. And that is the picture of whenever that was. I don't-- I was long gone when this-- when all this happened, and I didn't have to wear the Jewish star. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of our house in Laupheim. It  was a nice house, nothing very fancy. And the windows you see downstairs were the windows I climbed in and out all the time, because it took less time to climb into the window than it took to go up the stoop and walk through the door. And there was a nice little garden in the back. And there was a garden in the front, but it seems the garden in the front is now-- a house has been built on it. And I didn't know about that because I was long gone then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's a picture of me when I was about five years old. And I didn't like to have my picture taken either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken in Frankfurt on-- on one of my visits in my relatives' in Frankfurt where we went almost every year. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my grandmother on her 80th birthday, and around her are all her children, one son, some daughters, and all the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one at the top of the table. The old-- very old lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give her name? Give her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth Stern. This is a picture of my two brothers and myself. And as you can see, the brother is a lot-- the younger brother is a lot younger. He's about 12 years younger than I. And we loved him dearly. And I still love him a lot. My older brother, unfortunately, died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my brother's-- my older brother is Rudolph, and the younger one is Walter. This is a picture of my ninth grade graduation class, and I'm the only girl. I'm the only Jew among all these nice-looking gentlemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. In the school, most likely. I don't-- I don't know where it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Laupheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was the-- This is a picture of all the Jewish young folks in Laupheim. We had no place to train. This was taken in 1933, after all the stadiums and everything was forbidden for us. So we tried to straighten out a schoolyard next to the Jewish school so we could do some exercising there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm in this picture, the one with the pants on and the cap. I-- I always wore pants, ever since I was small. I don't what it was. I don't know why I wore it. I hated hats. This is a picture where I equaled the German record jumping in the Adolf Hitler stadium in Stuttgart, on June the 29th, I believe, 1936. And I was not very well received because I was the only Jew far and wide. No, Jew-- other Jewish athletes were there, and no Jewish spectators. This is the medal I got at that same competition. It has the swastika in there, and I'm sure the officials who had to give it-- give it to me were not very happy about it. This is a picture of my husband who, by then, was a captain in the United States Army. And he was the proudest captain in the whole United States Army. And he fought in Germany as a battalion surgeon and-- with the infantry-- and was shot at many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name? His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bruno. B. Lambert. This is a picture of my husband, myself, my oldest son, and my two grandchildren, Molly and Ben. My son's name is Glenn, and the-- this picture was taken in our backyard, maybe two years ago, which would make it-- what? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '93. This is a picture of our youngest son, Gary, and it was taken at our 50th wedding anniversary. And they were in for a lot of mischief. This is a picture of my mother at the age of 93. And in her arms she has her great-grandson, Jake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jake.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paula. Jake Bergmann is the grandson's name-- great-grandson's name.
